paraphrase: A joking line from the character Patrek that Jon will offer the white walkers 'hospitality' is read as a possible hint toward eventual diplomacy rather than pure war.
prior_failed_quote: Patrek of King's Mountain wore a cloak of blue and grey with silver flashing stars, his cloak thrown back to show his armor. He was not that strong, but he was brave, and he lasted longer than some who faced Wun Wun. When the giant brought his massive club down on his shoulder, the white wolf and blue lion both burst into flame.
prior_note: Joking line about Jon offering white walkers 'hospitality'; thread of potential diplomacy/peace rather than war

paraphrase: Precedent for humans striking deals with the Others: Craster trades his infant sons to the white walkers for safety; legend holds that a Lord Commander called the Night's King married a pale, blue-eyed woman and made sacrifices to the white walkers, possibly ending the first Long Night through a marriage pact rather than battle.
prior_failed_quote: Craster's Keep was a fortress of blackened wood and ancient stone, set hard against the side of a mountain. It squatted there like something obscene, a blight upon the landscape. Craster was a man of the free folk who made his living trading with both the wildlings and the Night's Watch, but he was also a creature of darkness who made sacrifices to the white walkers, trading them infant sons in exchange for peace and safety.
prior_note: Craster's pact with white walkers via baby sacrifice; precedent for potential compromise/deal with Others

paraphrase: In Book 3, Robb Stark considers legitimising Jon and writes a will that likely names Jon his heir as King in the North, though the books never reveal the will's exact contents.
prior_failed_quote: "A man is only as good as his word," Robb had said when they were boys. "And you are nothing if you cannot keep your oath." So Robb had kept his oath to the Freys, and it had cost him nothing but his life and his crown.
prior_note: Robb considers legitimising Jon as heir; will to name him King in the North drafted before death

paraphrase: Maege Mormont and Galbart Glover know about Robb's will and travel to Howland Reed in the Neck, who was present at the Tower of Joy when Jon was born and may know the truth of his parentage.
prior_failed_quote: Maege Mormont and Galbart Glover set off for Greywater Watch to meet Howland Reed. They knew that Robb had written his will, and they meant to see it honored. Howland Reed was present at the Tower of Joy when Rhaegar Targaryen fell, and if there was one man alive who knew the truth of Jon Snow's parentage, it would be him.
prior_note: Northern lords converging on Howland Reed with Robb's will naming Jon heir; Grand Northern Conspiracy thread

paraphrase: Maege's daughter Alysane Mormont serves with Stannis's army alongside the northern mountain clansmen, and two clan leaders visit Jon under circumstances he finds suspicious, suggesting a hidden purpose.
prior_failed_quote: "The north remembers," Roose Bolton said. "Some northmen have not forgotten Ned Stark's justice, nor the way he fell. They remember his wars, and his deeds, and they remember his bastard son who wears his face. If the rumors are true, if Jon Snow is truly a Stark, then he is the heir to the North by right of blood."
prior_note: 'The north remembers'; northern lords still loyal to Stark name and legacy; would support Jon as heir

paraphrase: The phrase 'The north remembers' is read as evidence the northern lords still revere Ned Stark's legacy and might rally to a Stark heir; Alys Karstark specifically comes to Jon for help because he is Ned Stark's son.
prior_failed_quote: "The north remembers," Roose Bolton said. "Some northmen have not forgotten Ned Stark's justice, nor the way he fell. They remember his wars, and his deeds, and they remember his bastard son who wears his face. If the rumors are true, if Jon Snow is truly a Stark, then he is the heir to the North by right of blood."
prior_note: "The north remembers"; evidence of northern loyalty to Stark legacy and Jon as heir candidate

paraphrase: Daenerys's House of the Undying vision of a blue flower growing from a wall of ice, filling the air with sweetness, is reread (via Lyanna's signature blue winter roses) as possibly symbolizing Jon, with the 'sweetness' read as foreshadowing a Jon/Dany romance.
prior_failed_quote: Somewhere there came the crying of a babe, though she knew not where.
prior_note: Daenerys's House of the Undying vision of blue flower on ice (Lyanna through winter roses imagery); foreshadows Jon/Dany meeting

paraphrase: Catelyn never accepts Jon, treating him coldly (sometimes cruelly) partly for political reasons -- protecting her son Robb's inheritance and worrying Jon could become a rival.
prior_failed_quote: If the boy had been a Stark, she might have loved him. She had tried, truly. But he was not a Stark, and so she could not. He was a bastard, and in the Riverlands, bastards bore the stigma of shame.
prior_note: Catelyn's cold treatment of Jon; her refusal to accept him as family

paraphrase: At Robert's visit to Winterfell, Catelyn seats Jon apart from the family at the back of the hall; realizing he has no inheritance or future at Winterfell, Jon tells Benjen he wants to join the Night's Watch.
prior_failed_quote: Winterfell's gates opened at Robert's call. Ned Stark rode north to meet the king's party, but before the gates could close behind him, Catelyn looked down from a tower window at the royal procession below. She recognized her husband, of course, and King Robert Baratheon on his great black destrier. But her eyes were drawn to the third figure, the one who rode beside the king in robes of crimson and gold.
prior_note: Robert's visit: Jon sees baseness of court hierarchy; realizes his bastard status excludes him from Stark succession

paraphrase: Reinforcements led by Alliser Thorne and disgraced former City Watch commander Janos Slynt arrive and accuse Jon of having joined the wildlings and murdered Qhorin, using his bastardy and worship of the old gods against him; they send him on what is effectively a suicide mission to kill Mance Rayder.
Riding north, Jon is upset to learn most Watch recruits are criminals sent as punishment, not the noble brotherhood he imagined; Tyrion Lannister bluntly corrects his idealism.
prior_failed_quote: That is a longsword, not an old man's cane. You think you had it hard, being a high lord's bastard? That boy Jeren is a septon's get, and Cotter Pyke is the baseborn son of a tavern wench. Now he commands Eastwatch by the Sea.
prior_note: Jon learns most Watch men are criminals, not noble recruits; Donal Noye expands his worldview about bastards.

paraphrase: Jon graduates hoping to become a ranger but is instead made steward to Commander Jeor Mormont; initially resentful, he accepts the role once Sam points out Jeor is grooming him for command.
prior_failed_quote: The Lord Commander had a broad, craggy face, a fringe of white hair, and the saddest eyes that Jon Snow had ever seen.
prior_note: Jon is made steward to Lord Commander Mormont rather than becoming a ranger; Mormont is grooming him for leadership.

paraphrase: When Benjen Stark goes missing beyond the Wall, Ghost finds two of his men dead with pale skin and blue eyes; that night, one corpse rises as a wight and Jon kills it with fire, burning his hand -- a wound he carries and reflects on for the rest of the story.
prior_failed_quote: The wight appeared to have targeted Commander Jeor specifically
prior_note: Jon encounters and kills risen wight after Benjen goes missing; Ghost finds his corpse.

paraphrase: The wight appears to have targeted Commander Jeor specifically, while the other reanimated corpse kills the acting First Ranger, suggesting the white walkers are deliberately targeting the Watch's leadership.
prior_failed_quote: The wight appears to have targeted Commander Jeor specifically
prior_note: White walkers deliberately target Watch leadership; Jeor nearly killed.

paraphrase: For saving Jeor's life, Jon is given the Valyrian steel bastard sword Longclaw, a Mormont family heirloom; Jeor gives it to Jon since his own son Jorah fled Westeros, symbolically making Jon his replacement heir, which leaves Jon conflicted since he'd once hoped Ned would give him a Stark blade.
prior_note: Jon receives Valyrian steel bastard sword as reward; Mormont's family heirloom symbolizes favor and succession.

paraphrase: Jon's Watch brothers bring him back before he deserts; Jeor Mormont tells him to grow up, arguing the war beyond the Wall against the dead matters more than the war in the south, and that Jon belongs at the Wall because of his Stark blood, his connection to the old gods, and his direwolf.
prior_failed_quote: Jeor tells Jon to grow up, war beyond Wall matters more than south
prior_note: Mormont argues dead matter more than southern politics; Jon belongs at Wall for Stark blood.

paraphrase: In Book 2, Jeor leads three hundred Watch men beyond the Wall to find Benjen, investigate abandoned wildling villages, and locate Mance Rayder's gathering host.
prior_note: Book 2 begins with Mormont's ranging beyond Wall to investigate abandoned villages and Mance Rayder.

paraphrase: At Craster's Keep, the incestuous wildling Craster -- who marries his own daughters and sacrifices infant sons to the white walkers -- is nonetheless treated as an ally by the Watch in exchange for shelter and intelligence.
prior_failed_quote: Craster trades sons to white walkers
prior_note: Craster's Keep revealed as wildling sanctuary that sacrifices infant sons to Others.
